nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes, lagoons, and sandy substrates adjacent to reefs in areas with bright light and moderate to strong surge; also occurs in more protected reef zones

decorPreferences: open placement away from stinging corals
space above colony for vertical extension

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae — derives most energy from light and has been reported to benefit from occasional supplemental feedings of fine powdered coral foods, live phytoplankton, or amino acids to support coloration and growth. Generally advised to feed 1–2 times per week as a supplement rather than a dietary requirement.

goodWith: non-stinging soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: stinging LPS corals in direct contact
corals with long sweeper tentacles within reach

summary: The Rumphella Coral is a photosynthetic Indo-Pacific gorgonian that forms tall, bushy, branching colonies. Generally considered intermediate to keep, it requires high non-laminar flow, moderate lighting, and stable reef water chemistry.
careNotes:
The Rumphella Coral is a zooxanthellate (photosynthetic) gorgonian from the Indo-Pacific — notably distinct from the non-photosynthetic Atlantic gorgonians that are notoriously difficult to keep. Because it hosts symbiotic zooxanthellae, which provide energy through photosynthesis, it is considerably more forgiving in captivity than its Atlantic counterparts.

A note on taxonomy: Rumphella has historically been placed in family Gorgoniidae by some sources, though current taxonomic authorities classify it in family Isididae within Order Alcyonacea. Either family name may be encountered in trade literature.

Key care requirements:
• Flow: High, non-laminar (alternating or surging) flow is essential — the branches should be observed swaying rather than pressed flat. Strong flow keeps fine branches free of detritus and delivers food particles to polyps. Laminar or uni-directional flow tends to cause tissue recession.
• Lighting: Generally advised to keep at moderate light levels (around 100–200 PAR). The coral can be placed lower in the tank and will grow upward toward higher light naturally. Acclimate new specimens to tank lighting gradually over 1–2 weeks to prevent bleaching.
• Placement: Middle of the rockwork provides a good compromise between adequate light and strong flow. The colony grows vertically and can reach 100 cm in the wild; in captivity, colonies have been reported reaching 30–60 cm given adequate space and time.
• Feeding: Occasional broadcast or target feedings of fine powdered coral food, live phytoplankton, or amino acids have been reported to improve growth rate and coloration, though these are not required for survival the way they are for non-photosynthetic gorgonians.
• Water chemistry: Stable alkalinity, calcium, and magnesium are important. Recommended ranges are kH 8–12 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm. Consistency matters more than hitting exact numbers.
• Aggression: Generally regarded as peaceful; however, other corals with sweeper tentacles or allelopathic chemicals can damage it. Spacing from aggressive stinging corals is advisable.
• Depth note: Wild records document Rumphella aggregata at 30–40 m depth, though the species complex is also collected from shallower reef zones (1–10 m). Aquacultured specimens — such as those from ORA Marshall Island stock — originate from shallow reef environments and generally adapt well to typical reef tank lighting.
• Fragging: Reported to propagate readily from branch cuttings mounted on frag plugs.
breedingNotes: Gorgonians reproduce sexually via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. In captivity, propagation is primarily achieved by fragging — cutting branches and mounting on frag plugs or rubble. Cut branches have been reported to encrust and grow successfully when given stable water conditions and adequate flow.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Rumphella Gorgonian Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 76–80°F / 24.4–26.7°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.025–1.026, kH 8–12, Ca 380–450 ppm, Mg 1200–1400 ppm), lighting (150–250 PAR), flow (medium-to-high), and care level (moderate).

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – ORA Rumphella Gorgonian
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (32–35 ppt / ~1.025), kH (8–12), flow (moderate-to-strong, alternating), and photosynthetic nature. Temperature range lower bound averaged with BRS and Manta Systems: adopted 23°C min.

additional.name: ReefStable – Gorgonian Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–80°F / 24–27°C), salinity (1.024–1.026), flow (medium-to-high surging), calcium (400–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1400 ppm), and feeding frequency (1–2×/week supplemental). Alkalinity 7–8 dKH noted here; BRS and AlgaeBarn agree on 8–12 (2v1), so 8–12 adopted.

additional.name: Coral Trait Database – Rumphella aggregata
additional.notes: Used for colony size (max colony height 1.00 m / 100 cm), depth range (30–40 m for R. aggregata), taxonomy (family Isididae, Class Octocorallia), and photosynthetic confirmation (P/R ratio 1.5, symbiont density 59 × 10³ polyp⁻¹).

additional.name: SaltwaterAquarium.com – ORA Marshall Island Rumphella Gorgonian
additional.notes: Confirmed photosynthetic nature, vertical bushy growth form, and peaceful temperament. Limited parameter data; used for qualitative care description.
